stats.headline: Pattern questions appear in nearly every P3–P6 paper. They look short but they're worth **1 to 5 marks** each — and the trickiest ones (interleaved sequences, two-colour figures, nth-term) are where strong students lose marks they shouldn't.

keyConcepts.title: Patterns: How important is this?
keyConcepts.body: We tracked **38 unique pattern questions** across P3–P6 WA, EOY, and Prelim papers in our bank. Even split between MCQ and OEQ, averaging **2.3 marks per question**.

pieChart.label: are open-ended (OEQ)
pieChart.caption: The other half are MCQ — but the MCQ traps are nastier.

bullets: **38 questions** across 28 different papers (P3–P6)
**50% OEQ, 50% MCQ** — short questions, but high stakes per mark
**Most common at P4** (15 Qs) and **P6** (12 Qs)

**We will cover the 6 trick families that unlock every pattern question:**
  • The Differences Method
  • Hidden sub-patterns
  • Find the nth term
  • Figure-based patterns
  • Two-colour figures
  • Shape composition
keyConcepts.callout: Patterns aren't on the official syllabus list — they hide inside Algebra, Basic operations, Statistics, and Geometry. That makes them easy to under-revise.

keyConcepts.title: 1: The Differences Method — always start here
keyConcepts.body: For ANY number sequence question, the first move is the same: **write the difference between each pair of consecutive numbers**. The rule almost always reveals itself.
bullets: **Step 1** — Subtract: each term minus the previous term. Write the differences below the sequence.
**Step 2** — Are the differences **constant**? (e.g. all +100) → it's a **linear pattern**. Just keep adding.
**Step 3** — Are the differences themselves **growing**? (e.g. +2, +4, +6, +8) → take the differences of those differences. If THAT's constant, the rule is a **doubled / squared** pattern.
**Step 4** — Don't forget to **check your rule** by extending one more term and seeing if it matches the next given number.
keyConcepts.callout: 9 out of 10 simple sequence questions fall to the Differences Method. Always start there.

keyConcepts.title: 2: Hidden sub-patterns — when the differences DON'T behave
keyConcepts.body: Some sequences look chaotic at first because **two or three different rules** are interleaved into ONE list. The PSLE / WA trick is to find the sub-pattern.
bullets: **Clue 1** — If the differences flip between negative AND positive (e.g. −2, −4, **+6**, −8, −10, **+12**), the rule is **'subtract, subtract, add'** that repeats.
**Clue 2** — If every 2nd / every 3rd number forms its own neat sequence, the original is **two or three interleaved sub-sequences**. Look at positions **1, 3, 5…** separately from **2, 4, 6…**
**Clue 3** — Always compare your answer to the **NEXT given number**. If your rule doesn't predict it correctly, your rule is wrong.

fullMarks.label: Full marks (the trick)
fullMarks.text: The differences are −3, −6, **+9**, −12, −15, **+18**, −21, −24, **+27**, … Pattern = **subtract, subtract, add**, with the gap growing by 3 each step. Last move was +27 → 923. Next move: −30 → **893**. Check: 893 − 33 = 860 ✓

keyConcepts.callout: If a sequence looks "random", the rule is hiding. Look for a REPEATING set of operations, not a single rule.

keyConcepts.title: 3: Find the nth term — divide, then look at the remainder
keyConcepts.body: "What is the **80th letter**?" or "What is the **74th bead**?" These questions look impossible because you'd have to write out 80 things. You don't. There's a 3-step shortcut.
bullets: **Step 1** — Find the **cycle length**. How many items make up ONE repeat of the pattern?
**Step 2** — Divide the position you want by the cycle length. Note the **remainder**.
**Step 3** — The remainder tells you which item in the cycle to land on. (If remainder = 0, the answer is the **last** item of the cycle.)

scoringExample.scenario: The pattern is **A B C D E F G** repeating. What is the **80th letter**?

oneMark.label: Brute force
oneMark.text: (Writing it out 80 times → easy to miscount)

fullMarks.label: Full marks
fullMarks.text: Cycle length = **7**. 80 ÷ 7 = **11 remainder 3**. So 80 is the **3rd letter** of the 12th cycle → **C**.

keyConcepts.callout: Remainder 0 = the **last** item in the cycle, NOT the first. The most common off-by-one mistake on nth-term questions.

keyConcepts.title: 4: Figure-based patterns — the longest section, because this is the hardest
keyConcepts.body: Figure-based patterns are the trickiest pattern questions on the paper. The diagram is full of dots / sticks / tiles / circles and it's easy to panic. The cure is a single discipline: **stop counting from the diagram. Build a TABLE.** Once the first 3-4 rows of the table are filled in, the formula reveals itself. Here are the 5 patterns you'll see most often, with worked examples.
bullets: **Pattern type A — Linear (constant difference)**. Each figure adds the SAME number of objects to the previous one. Formula: count = first + (n − 1) × d, where d is the constant difference.
**Pattern type B — Increasing difference / triangular numbers** (1, 3, 6, 10, 15…). Each figure adds ONE MORE than the previous one (+2, +3, +4, +5). Formula: count = n × (n + 1) ÷ 2.
**Pattern type C — Square numbers** (1, 4, 9, 16, 25…). Differences are consecutive ODD numbers (+3, +5, +7, +9). Formula: count = n². Or sometimes (n + 1)² depending on how the figures are labelled.
**Pattern type D — Two sub-counts** (e.g. dots increase by 3, lines increase by 4). Build TWO tables, find the rule for each, then add at the end if needed.

**The fixed 4-step method** for any figure question:
   • **Step 1** — Make a table: Figure no. | Count. Fill the first 3-4 rows from the diagram.
   • **Step 2** — Look at the **differences** between consecutive counts. Constant → Type A. Growing by 1 each step → Type B. Differences are consecutive odd numbers (3, 5, 7, 9) → Type C.
   • **Step 3** — Pick a formula candidate from the 5 types. Plug in n = 1, 2, 3 to **verify** it matches your table.
   • **Step 4** — Apply the formula to the target figure (Figure 10, Figure 20, Figure 50). Show the substitution clearly — markers want to see your working, not just the answer.
keyConcepts.callout: **The single most important habit:** before you do ANYTHING else, write the table. Three columns: Figure no. | Count | Difference. 90% of figure questions crack open the moment that table is on the page. The remaining 10% need you to split into TWO tables (one per colour / object) and apply the same method to each.

keyConcepts.title: Now let's see it in action — 3 real worked examples
keyConcepts.body: The theory is one thing. The marking scheme doesn't care if you know the 5 pattern types — it cares about the **working**. So we'll apply the same 4-step method to 3 real prelim / WA questions, with the original diagrams. Each one shows the **exact question**, the **table** to draw, and the **formula** that falls out.
bullets: **Coming up:** growing-sum (beads in rows), square numbers (tiles in a grid), and shape composition (4 triangles joined together).
**Same recipe every time** — read the question → build the table → list the differences → derive the formula → verify → apply to the target figure.
keyConcepts.callout: Pause and try each one on paper before reading the answer. The point isn't to memorise the result — it's to build the habit.

keyConcepts.title: Worked example 1 — Beads in rows (increasing difference)
keyConcepts.body: **P5 Tao Nan EOY 2023.** Beads are arranged in rows: row n contains n beads. Figure 1 is just row 1, Figure 2 is rows 1-2, Figure 3 is rows 1-3, and so on. **Find the TOTAL number of beads (grey + white) in Figure 10.**
bullets:
**Step 1 — Read the current table from the diagram.** Count all the beads in each figure shown.
   • Figure 1 → 1 bead
   • Figure 2 → 3 beads (1 + 2)
   • Figure 3 → 6 beads (1 + 2 + 3)
   • Figure 4 → 10 beads (1 + 2 + 3 + 4)

**Step 2 — Extend the table by working out the next rows.** Differences: 3 − 1 = **2**, 6 − 3 = **3**, 10 − 6 = **4**. **The difference INCREASES by 1 each step** (next: +5, +6, +7…).
   • Figure 5 → 10 + 5 = **15 beads**
   • Figure 6 → 15 + 6 = **21 beads**
   • Figure 7 → 21 + 7 = **28 beads**
   • Figure 8 → 28 + 8 = **36 beads**
   • Figure 9 → 36 + 9 = **45 beads**
   • Figure 10 → 45 + 10 = **55 beads**

**Step 3 — Derive the formula.** Figure n total = 1 + 2 + 3 + … + n. Use the **sum formula** (first + last) × count ÷ 2.
   • Total in Figure n = (1 + n) × n ÷ 2 = **n × (n + 1) ÷ 2**.
   • Check F4: 4 × 5 ÷ 2 = 10 ✓. F10: 10 × 11 ÷ 2 = 55 ✓.
**Step 4 — Answer.** Figure 10 → 10 × 11 ÷ 2 = **55 beads**. ✓ matches the row we extended in Step 2.
keyConcepts.callout: **Take-away:** when each step ADDS one more than the previous (+2, +3, +4, +5…), the total is a **triangular number** and the shortcut formula is **n × (n + 1) ÷ 2**. No need to add 10 numbers one at a time on the exam.

keyConcepts.title: Worked example 2 — Squares in a grid (squared differences)
keyConcepts.body: **P6 Rosyth WA2 2023, 3 marks.** Squares form a growing n × n grid (Figure 1 is 1×1, Figure 2 is 2×2, and so on). **How many small squares in total are in Figure 8?**
bullets:
**Step 1 — Read the current table from the diagram.**
   • Figure 1 → 1 square
   • Figure 2 → 4 squares
   • Figure 3 → 9 squares
   • Figure 4 → 16 squares
   • Figure 5 → 25 squares

**Step 2 — Extend the table by working out the next rows.** Differences: 4 − 1 = **3**, 9 − 4 = **5**, 16 − 9 = **7**, 25 − 16 = **9**. Differences are growing by 2 — **consecutive odd numbers**.
   • Figure 6 → 25 + 11 = **36 squares**
   • Figure 7 → 36 + 13 = **49 squares**
   • Figure 8 → 49 + 15 = **64 squares**

**Step 3 — Derive the formula.** Consecutive odd-number differences is the **fingerprint of square numbers**. Check the totals against n × n.
   • 1 × 1 = 1 ✓, 2 × 2 = 4 ✓, 3 × 3 = 9 ✓, 4 × 4 = 16 ✓, 5 × 5 = 25 ✓.
   • Formula: **Total in Figure n = n × n = n²**.
**Step 4 — Answer.** Figure 8 total = 8 × 8 = **64 squares**. ✓ matches the row we extended in Step 2.
keyConcepts.callout: **Take-away:** when the differences are consecutive odd numbers (3, 5, 7, 9…), the totals are square numbers. Differences-of- differences = 2 — the calling card of n².

keyConcepts.title: Worked example 3 — Figure: shapes (4 triangles joined)
keyConcepts.body: **P6 ACS-J Prelim 2024, 2 marks.** Figure 1 is one isosceles triangle with perimeter **40 cm**. Figure 2 is made from **4** such triangles arranged to share their bases. The perimeter of Figure 2 is **112 cm**. Find the length of the base PQ.
bullets:
**Step 1 — Identify outer vs inner sides.** Look at Figure 2 closely.
   • Each triangle has 3 sides: 2 equal legs + 1 base PQ.
   • The 4 bases all face inward and are SHARED — they don't appear in the perimeter.
   • Only the **8 legs** (2 per triangle × 4 triangles) appear on the outside.

**Step 2 — Set up the equation.** All 8 legs are equal length (the triangles are identical).
   • Figure 2 perimeter = 8 × leg = 112 cm
   • Solve: leg = 112 ÷ 8 = **14 cm**.

**Step 3 — Use Figure 1 to find the base.** Figure 1 is just one triangle.
   • Perimeter = 2 legs + 1 base = 40 cm
   • Substitute: 2(14) + base = 40 → 28 + base = 40 → **base PQ = 12 cm**.
**Step 4 — Verify.** Triangle has sides 14, 14, 12 → perimeter 40 ✓. Figure 2 has 8 outer legs of 14 → perimeter 112 ✓.

scoringExample.scenario: Why does 112 ÷ 4 = 28 NOT work as the answer?

oneMark.label: The trap
oneMark.text: Dividing by 4 (number of triangles) assumes each triangle contributes one outer side to the perimeter. It doesn't — it contributes **2 outer sides** (both legs). Always count the outer edges first.

fullMarks.label: General rule
fullMarks.text: For any composite shape: number of outer edges in Figure 2 = (sides per piece × number of pieces) − (2 × number of shared internal joins). Internal sides cancel because they're counted from BOTH pieces.

keyConcepts.callout: **Take-away:** in shape composition, the FIRST move is always "count the outer edges". Everything else falls out from there.

keyConcepts.title: Let's get some practice!
keyConcepts.body: Time to try some real pattern questions from P4–P6 school papers. Mix of MCQ and OEQ across every flavour we just covered.
bullets: **6 MCQ** + **4 OEQ** — a real cross-section of pattern questions from the bank.
After you submit, the AI flags the trick families you got wrong and suggests a follow-up focused quiz on those.
Mistakes are saved — we'll return to them in an auto-revision session in **1 week**.

cta.label: Click here to start your practice quiz

keyWords.definition: Each individual number / item in a sequence. The 1st term, 2nd term, etc.
The term at position n. 'Find the 80th letter' = find the term at the 80th position.
A repeating group of items. In 'A B C A B C A B C', the cycle is 'A B C' with cycle length 3.
Subtract one term from the previous one. Differences often reveal the rule of a sequence.
What's left after a division. Key tool for nth-term questions: 80 ÷ 7 = 11 remainder 3.
Next to each other in a sequence. 5, 6, 7 are consecutive whole numbers.
Every step adds (or subtracts) the same amount. Simplest kind of pattern.
The label of each step in a figure-based pattern. Figure 1, Figure 2, Figure 3...

subTopics.label: Patterns: constant difference
subTopics.description: Number sequences where every step adds (or subtracts) the same amount. Linear add/subtract patterns plus place-value rebuilds (e.g. 12 608 = 10 000 + 2000 + ? + 8). Solved by writing the differences between consecutive terms and confirming they're all equal.

subTopics.label: Patterns: changing difference
subTopics.description: Sequences where the differences themselves change — they flip sign (subtract-subtract-add repeats), grow each step (triangular / square / cubic), or hide two interleaved sub-sequences in one list. Trick is to find the repeating sub-rule or look at every-other term.

subTopics.label: Patterns: string of symbols
subTopics.description: Repeating sequences of letters / beads / shapes / colours. Questions ask 'what is the 80th letter / 74th bead / 105th term?'. Solved by finding the cycle length, dividing the target position, and using the remainder.

subTopics.label: Figure patterns
subTopics.description: 'Figure 1, Figure 2, Figure 3…' with objects (tiles, dots, beads, circles, squares) growing. Asks for the count in Figure n, OR the total area / perimeter. Solved by tabling the first 3-4 figures, listing differences, and matching to a formula (linear, triangular, n², or sum of two squares).

subTopics.label: Shape patterns
subTopics.description: A bigger figure built from N identical triangles, squares, or rectangles. Tests perimeter and area reasoning where internal shared edges cancel out. Only outer edges contribute to perimeter; total area = N × (area of one piece).
